name: Properties of Operations — The Rules That Save Time
whatIsIt: Operations follow handy rules. COMMUTATIVE: order doesn't matter for + and × (3 + 5 = 5 + 3; 3 × 4 = 4 × 3). ASSOCIATIVE: grouping doesn't matter (2 + (3 + 4) = (2 + 3) + 4). DISTRIBUTIVE: 3 × (10 + 2) = 3×10 + 3×2. IDENTITY: + 0 and × 1 change nothing. ZERO: anything × 0 = 0. These let you rearrange to calculate the easy way.
whyNeeded: These rules turn hard sums into easy ones — reorder to make friendly pairs, break a big multiplication into parts, or spot a shortcut. They're also the hidden logic behind mental maths and all of algebra later.
realLifeUses: Adding a bill in any order and getting the same total
Buying 6 packs of ₹12 as 6×10 + 6×2 in your head

vocabulary.meaning: Order doesn't change the answer for + and ×.
a × (b + c) = a×b + a×c — spread the multiply over the sum.
Adding 0 or multiplying by 1 leaves a number unchanged.

story.title: Meena's Quick Total
story.text: Anna asks Meena to add 7 + 6 + 3 fast. Meena swaps the order: 7 + 3 first makes a friendly 10, then + 6 = 16. 'You changed the order!' says Anna. 'For addition I'm allowed — that's the commutative rule, and it makes tens I can add in my head,' Meena grins.
story.extractedProblem: Add 7 + 6 + 3 the easy way. What is the total, and which pair makes it simple?

visual.caption: Turn the array and it's the same 12: 3 × 4 = 4 × 3. Order doesn't change a product (commutative).

teachingGallery.title: See the rules
teachingGallery.note: Reorder, regroup, or spread out — the answer stays the same.

standardMethod.summary: Use the rules to rearrange into an easier calculation, then work it out.
steps: Commutative: swap the order of + or × to make friendly numbers (pairs of ten, easy products).
Associative: regroup with brackets to add/multiply the easy pair first.
Distributive: break one factor into parts — a × (b + c) = a×b + a×c.

commonMistakes.mistake: Thinking subtraction and division are also commutative (7 − 3 = 3 − 7).
commonMistakes.fix: Only + and × are commutative. For − and ÷ the order DOES matter: 7 − 3 ≠ 3 − 7.

commonMistakes.mistake: In a × (b + c), multiplying only the first part (a×b) and forgetting a×c.
commonMistakes.fix: Spread the multiply over BOTH parts: a × b AND a × c, then add.

challenge.q: Ravi says 10 − 4 is the same as 4 − 10. Is he right?

options.label: No — subtraction order matters, they aren't equal

challenge.answer: No — subtraction order matters, they aren't equal

teachBackPrompt: Show a grown-up a quick mental-maths trick using one property — like reordering 8 + 7 + 2 to make a ten, or breaking 6 × 12 into 6×10 + 6×2 — and explain which rule lets you do it.

revisionCard.summary: Commutative: order free for + and × (not − or ÷). Associative: regroup the easy pair first. Distributive: a × (b + c) = a×b + a×c. Identity: + 0 and × 1 change nothing. Zero: anything × 0 = 0. Use them to make sums easy.

rememberIt.hook: Three laws: Commutative (swap), Associative (regroup), Distributive (share out)
rememberIt.unpack: a+b = b+a. (a+b)+c = a+(b+c). a(b+c) = ab+ac. They make mental maths easy.

studentQuestions.a: You can swap the order: a+b = b+a and a x b = b x a. (Not for subtraction or division!)
Multiplying over a bracket: a(b+c) = ab + ac. 6x13 = 6x10 + 6x3 = 78 - a great mental trick.
